citation: Mon, L. M. (2012). Professional avatars: Librarians and educators in virtual worlds. Journal of Documentation, 68(3), 318-329. https://doi.org/10.1108/00220411211225566
summary:
Mon examines librarianship and education in virtual world settings, focusing on how librarians and educators establish professional identity and trust while teaching and providing information services as avatars in Second Life. The study treats virtual worlds as highly graphical, immersive, and "game-like" settings where the professional self is not only textual or institutional but visibly embodied. For GBLS, this article helps explain why library work inside game worlds or game-adjacent virtual platforms requires more than simply opening a virtual branch: staff must manage avatar appearance, spatial cues, credibility, patron expectations, and platform culture.

The study uses a qualitative, ethnographic approach. Mon spent more than a year and a half as a participant observer in Second Life librarian and educator communities, learning the platform through roles such as patron, librarian, student, educator, group member, event organizer, meeting leader, conference attendee, tour leader, and virtual workplace builder. The interview phase included 12 in-world semi-structured interviews with seven librarians and five educators, conducted in Second Life workplaces or research facilities, along with visits to 50 virtual world workplaces. The analysis is framed through Goffman's concepts of presentation of self, face, role, setting, frame, and control over information and conversation.

The article's central finding is that librarians and educators had to establish professional identity, credibility, and trust for two different audiences: "newbies" and "residents." Newer users could be reassured by conservative, humanoid, friendly, professional avatar appearances and familiar workplace imagery such as books, bookshelves, computers, classrooms, and reference desks. Experienced Second Life residents, however, often read avatar quality as cultural competence. For them, a default or visibly novice avatar could reduce credibility, so librarians and educators sometimes purchased higher-quality skins, clothing, animations, and accessories to signal that they belonged in the virtual world.

Mon's findings are especially useful because they show that virtual-world library service is embodied and performative. Librarians and educators described wanting avatars that would be "taken seriously," and female avatars often struggled to find professional clothing in a marketplace oriented toward entertainment and display. Some participants changed avatar gender, ethnicity, or style depending on community expectations. Others used avatars as part of learning scenarios, historical reenactments, or case-study performances, making the avatar itself part of the informational environment rather than merely a pointer to a real-world professional.

The study also shows how virtual work unsettled traditional library space. In the 50 observed Second Life libraries, common features included photos and posters, books and bookshelves, computers, and reference desks. These cues helped visitors recognize scholarly purpose and trust that they were interacting with a "real" librarian, but participants also questioned whether virtual libraries should simply rebuild physical libraries. Some worked anywhere rather than at a stable desk, served international users across time zones and languages, used translation tools, placed reference desks outside buildings, or experimented with open areas and nontraditional environments. The key problem was not whether Second Life could imitate a library, but how visible setting and avatar cues could frame credible information interactions.

Alternative identity is another important finding. Five of the 12 participants maintained separate "alt" identities. Alts could be used for educational videos, role-playing characters, backup inventory, research, money management, or uninterrupted work when a main professional avatar was visible to contacts and likely to receive instant messages. This helps GBLS think about staff presence in online game worlds: persistent identity can build relationships, but it also creates interruption, privacy, labor, and boundary-management issues.

For the larger GBLS project, Mon contributes to the virtual-platform strand rather than to game programming narrowly. The article does not study a game night, game collection, or designed game-based learning program. Its relevance is that Second Life was widely perceived as a game or game-like environment, and librarians had to make professional service legible within that frame. Mon therefore gives the review vocabulary for avatar-mediated service, professional identity, credibility, trust, workplace staging, and platform-native expectations. These issues remain relevant for libraries considering services inside Minecraft, Roblox, social VR, Meta Horizon Worlds, Discord-linked game communities, or other persistent virtual environments where library staff appear through avatars, handles, or designed spaces.

The article's limits are also clear. It is based on a small interview sample from 2008 and a platform moment that has changed substantially. It does not measure patron outcomes, assess long-term service sustainability, or compare virtual-world library services with other online channels. Still, its ethnographic detail makes it one of the stronger sources for understanding the social mechanics of library work in game-like virtual environments, especially the tension between being recognizably professional and being fluent enough in the platform culture to be trusted by residents.

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Programming Formats and Service Models` > `## Programs Inside Game Worlds and Virtual Platforms`, immediately after the Webber and Nahl (2011) paragraph and before Chow et al. (2012). Proposed text: "Mon (2012) adds a staff-facing account of avatar-mediated professionalism in Second Life. Through participant observation, 12 in-world interviews with librarians and educators, and visits to 50 virtual workplaces, Mon shows that virtual-world library service depended on professional avatar presentation, trust cues, and spatial staging: conservative humanoid avatars and familiar bookshelves or reference desks helped reassure newcomers, while polished avatar appearance signaled credibility to experienced residents. For GBLS, the study explains why services inside game-like worlds require platform fluency, identity management, and credible staff presence, not only a virtual room or collection."

# Jedlicka, C. M., & Eaton, M. E. (2024). Creating a homemade mobile augmented reality game in a community college library: An open source approach. *Journal of Web Librarianship*. https://doi.org/10.1080/19322909.2024.2391851

Unique identifier: `jedlickaCreatingHomemadeMobile2024`; Zotero item key `CQK5X98J`; attachment key `7KHWCHSP`.

Jedlicka and Eaton (2024) describe how librarians at Kingsborough Community College built a mobile augmented reality orientation game for new students using open-source tools and existing library infrastructure. The project began from a practical outreach problem: the library wanted a fun, welcoming activity that would orient students to library locations and services, reduce unease about the academic library, and provide a positive early experience. Instead of adopting a commercial AR platform, the librarians built a browser-based Web AR game using AR.js, A-Frame, JavaScript, and LibGuides.

The article is especially valuable because it documents the constraints behind the design. KCC is a public community college library with no budget for the activity, limited staff time, no large programming team, weak Wi-Fi, and a two-month launch window. Those constraints made a commercial app less attractive: students might need to download another app, storage and Wi-Fi could become barriers, vendors might change terms or discontinue platforms, and subscription costs might not fit library values or budgets. An open-source, browser-based approach let the game load once, run client-side without network calls during play, avoid app downloads, and remain shareable through openly available code.

The game itself was a self-guided AR tour. Students scanned a QR code, enabled their phone camera, and walked around the library looking for printed visual markers. Scanning each marker triggered a virtual shape and checked off a location in a drop-down progress list. After all eight markers were found, students saw a congratulations screen and returned to librarians for a small candy reward and praise. The locations were limited to the library's second floor, where service points and student-centered amenities such as the Reference Desk, Circulation, computers, printers, leisure books, and study rooms were located. A donated tablet and a paper checklist were available for students without compatible devices or for accessibility support.

The results are modest but concrete. In initial drop-in beta testing during two afternoon reference shifts, 12 students completed the activity and a similar number began but did not finish, often because they passed by with a class that lacked time. Slow Wi-Fi sometimes delayed the initial load, but once the game loaded it ran smoothly; 10 of 12 completers used their own phones and two used the library tablet. Students quickly adapted to the game, mostly ignored the paper checklist, and gave positive comments such as "fun," "cool," "pretty clear," and "pretty entertaining." One student said the activity helped him get to know the library and discover study rooms.

The second phase changed the service model. When librarians integrated the AR activity into introductory library instruction sessions, completion and engagement improved substantially: all 34 students across three sessions completed the activity. Student and faculty feedback remained positive, and students described the activity as engaging, fun, a good starter for new people, and useful even for someone who had been enrolled for years but did not know everything in the library. The authors conclude that the AR game worked better as a class-based activity than as an elective standalone game.

For the larger GBLS project, this source contributes practical implementation knowledge. It shows that AR orientation does not require a commercial platform or professional developer, but it does require a technically curious librarian with JavaScript skill, planning time, iterative testing, device contingencies, marker printing, staff facilitation, and a willingness to accept imperfect technology. It also shows how game-based orientation can be tied to actual library space rather than abstract service information. The strongest design lesson is to match the game format to the workflow: a self-guided drop-in activity may seem flexible, but classroom integration can produce better participation and completion.

The limitations are significant. Data collection was informal, based on observation and brief verbal feedback rather than formal surveys, learning tests, anxiety measures, or long-term use data. Accessibility remained a concern because AR is heavily visual and paper workarounds are not equivalent to the AR experience. Technical sustainability is also unresolved: JavaScript libraries change quickly, AR.js development has slowed, LibGuides analytics were limited, and future functionality would require more coding. Even so, the case is useful because it makes the hidden infrastructure of a small, open-source GBLS project visible.

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy`, after the Intawong, Puritat, Leenaraj, and Arayaphan (2023) paragraph. Proposed text: "Jedlicka and Eaton (2024) add a low-budget community-college AR orientation case: Kingsborough librarians built a browser-based scavenger-hunt-style tour with AR.js, A-Frame, JavaScript, and LibGuides rather than a commercial app. Students scanned markers around the library to reveal virtual shapes and check off service locations; informal feedback was positive, but the activity worked better when embedded in library instruction sessions than as a standalone drop-in game."

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Programming Formats and Service Models` > `## VR, AR, and Immersive Technology`, after the Ponto and Waddle (2023) paragraph. Proposed text: "Jedlicka and Eaton (2024) show that immersive GBLS can be small, browser-based, and locally built. Their Web AR tour avoided app downloads, minimized Wi-Fi dependence after initial load, used existing LibGuides infrastructure, and published code openly, but it also required JavaScript skill, marker testing, device backup, accessibility workarounds, and ongoing attention to open-source maintainability."

Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Match Game Form to Service Goal`, immediately after the heading. Proposed text: "Jedlicka and Eaton (2024) offer a concrete form-fit lesson: an AR scavenger hunt can orient students to physical locations, but participation depends on the surrounding service workflow. Their game was technically playable as a self-guided drop-in activity, yet completion and engagement improved when it became part of scheduled library instruction."

Professional avatars: librarians and educators in virtual worlds
Lorri M. Mon
College of Communication & Information, Florida State University, Tallahassee, Florida, USA
Abstract
Purpose – This study aims to examine librarianship and education in virtual world settings, focusing particularly on how librarians and educators establish professional identity and trust while teaching and providing information services as avatars within Second Life. Design/methodology/approach – Data was gathered through semi-structured in-depth interviews with 12 librarians and educators within the virtual world of Second Life, and in visits to 50 virtual world workplaces. Findings – Librarians and educators faced challenges of establishing professional identity and credibility for two different audiences – i.e. “newbies” and “residents” – within the “game-like” virtual world environment. Visual elements such as a conservative, humanoid avatar appearance, and imagery drawn from traditional workplaces were used by librarians and educators to establish their professional role with “newbies”, while for “residents” an upgraded avatar appearance was necessary to convey credibility. Practical implications – For librarians and educators working within highly visual, graphical online settings such as virtual worlds, this study describes issues in providing avatar-mediated online distance education and online library services. Originality/value – This research provides in-depth interviews and observations with librarians and educators from a variety of libraries and educational settings. The study also explores avatar-mediated communication, which is little-examined as yet in LIS research.
Keywords Virtual worlds, Second Life, Avatars, Digital reference, Distance education, Librarians, Distance learning
Paper type Research paper
1. Introduction
Today’s librarians and educators stand at the crossroads of two worlds – teaching and answering questions within “brick and mortar” libraries and classrooms while also handling the “virtual branch” or “virtual classroom” for a growing audience of online users. In the USA alone, 3.9 million students enrolled in online courses in Fall 2007, increasing to 5.6 million by Fall 2009 (Allen and Seaman, 2008, 2010). Libraries support “virtual” users via e-mail, chat, instant messaging and other online technologies used among more than 12 million reference transactions handled by US research libraries during 2007-2008 (Kyrillidou and Bland, 2009). Keeping up with new technologies in teaching and answering questions for online students is an ongoing challenge for librarians and educators.
The current issue and full text archive of this journal is available at www.emeraldinsight.com/0022-0418.htm
The author would like to thank ASIS&T SIG-USE for the support provided for this research through the 2007 Elfreda A. Chatman Research Grant Award.

 In exploring possibilities beyond text-based online communication, educators and librarians have experimented with virtual worlds such as Second Life (see www. secondlife.com). Second Life (SL) users enter a graphical, visually rich immersive environment in which the real-world self is represented by an online avatar that can speak, emote, gesture, and even rise into the air and fly. This virtual “self” is infinitely customizable and can appear as human, animal, mechanized, or an inanimate object. As early as 2002, educators experimented with teaching classes in Second Life (Mon, 2009). Second Life Library 2.0, today known as the Community Virtual Library, launched in April 2006, and by 2007 librarians working as avatars on the Info Islands had answered 6,769 virtual reference questions (Alliance Library System, 2008; Bell et al., 2007). Also by 2007, more than 70 colleges, universities, and schools had created an in-world presence (Jennings and Collins, 2007), establishing a building, teaching location, or campus. Professional education work in a virtual world involves creating a personal avatar and learning to use it while teaching and communicating information. This article examines virtual world communication in educational contexts, seeking to understand the “professional as avatar” and its impact on the professional work of librarians and educators in virtual worlds.
2. Background
Most research on educators and librarians in Second Life involves descriptive case studies of classes (Delwiche, 2006; Jarmon et al., 2008; Kemp, 2008; Sanchez, 2009) or case studies of individual libraries. (Buckland and Godfrey, 2010; Czarnecki, 2008; Godfrey, 2008; Hill and Lee, 2009; Swanson, 2007) As yet, few researchers have surveyed or interviewed educators and librarians working in virtual worlds (Luo, 2008; Luo and Kemp, 2008; New Media Consortium, 2007). Luo (2008) surveyed 21 reference volunteer librarians about their work with the Second Life reference service, finding that the majority answered questions on Second Life topics, but fewer received non-Second Life related questions. Luo and Kemp (2008) surveyed 25 educators about teaching LIS students in Second Life, learning that the majority used Second Life for class activities, exercises and assignments rather than for lectures, and that many used the virtual world for group exercises and team-building, and as a place for students to give presentations. A survey of 209 educators, librarians and students by New Media Consortium (2007) found that 57 respondents reported teaching in the virtual world, and 103 stated that their institution either owned a virtual island or rented land in-world. Two other methods used by researchers were comprehensive reviews of the research literature, and unobtrusive observations. Mon (2010) conducted an in-depth review of the research literature to provide a detailed account of Second Life teaching methods. Jennings and Collins (2007, p. 181) observed workplaces of 70 educational institutions in Second Life, finding that information was commonly communicated to visitors via visual displays such as signs (76.1 percent) or clickable web links (46.5 percent). The most comprehensive review of library workplaces was a sample of eight public libraries observed by Stimpson (2009), who identified potentially promising best practices such as offering library events, group memberships, gifts, and tutorials.

 3. Purpose of study
Notably missing from the literature was exploration by LIS researchers of the impact of “avatar-mediated communication” (Castronova, 2003) on the professional work of librarians and educators. Second Life supports text chat, instant messaging and audio, and embodies librarians and educators as avatars with the ability to “recreate themselves” with any desired appearance as well as to modify their graphically rendered surroundings. This raises the key question: how does avatar-mediated communication influence professional information interactions conducted by librarians and educators in Second Life? While some research has explored the impact of avatar self-representation on users (Boellstorff, 2008; Yee and Bailenson, 2007; Lee and Hoadley, 2006), only limited research has focused on educator and librarian avatar embodiment in a professional, working context (New Media Consortium, 2007). Improved understanding of the dynamics of professional identity in virtual worlds would support librarians and educators in more effectively utilizing highly graphical digital and virtual world environments for teaching and information services in future.
4. Method
This study utilized a qualitative, ethnographic approach of immersion in Second Life as a participant observer, followed by workplace observations and semi-structured in-world interviews with librarians and educators. Preparation began by joining Second Life in August 2006 as a participant observer, spending five or more hours per week for over a year and a half within the librarian and educator communities prior to the interview phase in May 2008. The participant observer phase included learning about librarians and educators in Second Life through direct experiences such as asking questions as a patron and answering questions as a librarian, attending classes as a student and teaching as an educator; and undertaking other roles in librarian and educator communities including audience member, group member, group leader, event organizer, meeting leader, conference attendee, conference planner, conference presenter, tour leader, tour follower, campus workplace builder and library builder. This immersive involvement facilitated preparation for interviews through acquiring functional skills and knowledge of the history, culture, and language of the virtual world’s users. Twelve interviews were conducted from May 2008 to December 2008 with seven librarians and five educators either at their Second Life workplaces, or at an in-world research facility. During interviews, avatars sat on sofas or chairs around a recording device. The device recorded only participants who willingly touched the device to indicate their consent after being informed about the study and their rights as research participants. Following one-hour semi-structured interviews, photographs were taken of avatars in their Second Life workplaces or at the research facility. Photos were also taken of librarian and educator workplace settings, and any available information objects were collected. Fifty in-world library workplaces were visited between May 2008 and January 2009. Data analysis was conducted in two phases:
(1) In Phase I, a first-level analysis explored how librarians and educators answered questions and provided information in Second Life (Mon, 2009). This

 initial study described work activities and information tools used by librarians and educators, types of questions answered, how they provided information, and improved functionalities that they would have liked to have in better managing information work in Second Life.
(2) In Phase II, the current study, a secondary-level analysis focused on the avatar-mediated aspect of librarians’ and educators’ professional work in Second Life – the visualized self-representations of librarians, educators, and their professional workplaces, and the impact of these visualized representations on information work and teaching. Emerging in this second phase of the study was an issue uniquely challenging to librarians and educators working within virtual worlds – establishing professional identity, credibility and trust while embodied as an avatar in a highly visual, “game-like” setting.
Data analysis was guided by a theoretical framework drawing upon the work of sociologist Erving Goffman on presentation of the self (Goffman, 1959). Goffman’s work has influenced virtual world researchers including Carr (2008) in research with students in Second Life, Boostrom (2008) on virtual identity for new users in Second Life, Delwiche (2006, p.164) on identity within Everquest, and McDonough (1999) on virtual identity in research with designers of virtual worlds. Goffman’s work was grounded in symbolic interactionism and focused on impression management and presentation of the self within interpersonal interactions. (Blumer, 1969; Goffman, 1959) In Goffman’s view, interpersonal interactions were performative, staged and framed within each participant’s expectations about the setting, their socially-defined roles, and actions perceived as appropriate for the encounter. Individuals maintain multiple public roles or “selves”, selectively presenting the “self” appropriate for each audience (Goffman, 1961). Within this social performance of identity, one could “lose face” when not treated with appropriate respect during an encounter (Goffman, 1955). Individuals may selectively reveal or conceal information about the self, the “information preserve”, and seek to exert control over who can summon him or her into conversation, or the “conversational preserve” (Goffman, 1997a, p. 140; Goffman, 1997b, pp. 50-1). Goffman’s conceptions of frame, setting, roles, performative identity, stigma, face, the information preserve and conversational preserve provided the framework for data analysis. Member-checking was used in discussing findings with study participants, and in presenting results and inviting comments at real world and in-world conferences and discussion groups attended by Second Life librarians and educators. It should be noted that the generalizability or transferability of these results is limited by the small sample size as well as the highly changeable nature of virtual environments over time. As will be discussed in the conclusions, virtual world builders sometimes introduce key modifications to these environments, and particularly in response to findings of researchers.
5. Findings
5.1 The professional as avatar
Any self-representation that can be imagined can be created in Second Life. Asked about unusual avatars they had seen, librarians and educators described everything from animals and insects (“a giant beetle”, “a squirrel”, “a butterfly”) to monsters and imaginary creatures (“a tiny fairy”, “a dragon”, “a frog prince”, “a flying spaghetti

 monster”) and inanimate objects (“a shiny, floating sphere”, “a box”, “glowing neon tubes”, “a puff of smoke”). Developing professional aplomb regarding user appearance is helpful. One example of matter-of-fact acceptance of unusual avatar self-representations was described by a librarian: “I once had a reference interview with an avatar who had a bloody sword piercing his abdomen [. . .] He never mentioned it, nor did I”. Librarians and educators sought to craft their avatar self-representations in ways that would facilitate professional communication. A commonly expressed sentiment was, “I want an avatar that will be taken seriously”, and specific attributes mentioned included “humanoid”, “conservative”, “friendly and professional”. One educator made a distinction regarding appearance and different audiences: “avatars that don’t know me might not take me seriously when I am a monkey or a bear”. Although in the real world (“real life” or “RL”), clothing has both utilitarian functions and performative aspects, virtual world clothing is almost entirely performative. To convey a serious, scholarly, and professional avatar self-representation, educators and librarians wore business suits, eyeglasses, and conservative clothing. For female avatars, simply finding professional clothing was a challenge, with bikinis and ball gowns more easily found than business suits. A librarian noted, “I did try to find some more business-like clothing in SL, but it was hard. Most clothing is rather extreme [. . .] low cut, party dresses, very short skirts, tight tops”. Second Life is perceived as a “game” by many users, making it unexpected to encounter working professionals within this context (Pence, 2007/2008). Although clothing stores were established early (Ondrejka, 2004), available designs primarily support entertainment. As another librarian commented, “I work to appear un-barbiedoll-like”. Goffman (1956, p. 269) mentions “audience segregation” in which a different self may be acted out for different audiences. For educators and librarians in Second Life, avatar and clothing choices were influenced by cultural expectations of two major audiences of virtual world users – “newbies”, who are the newer arrivals (sometimes derogatorily called “n00bs”), and “residents”,, who are more seasoned participants who “live” and “work” in Second Life, perhaps even renting or owning land within the virtual world. A librarian commented about choosing a conventional and conservative humanoid appearance: “I didn’t want to unnerve anyone who was new to SL”. Another librarian said, “I wanted to be accessible, unintimidating to newcomers”. However, residents posed another challenge for professional self-presentation. Residents typically present a more “polished” avatar appearance due to significant time, effort and/or money invested into self-created or designer-made clothing, adornments, “photo-realistic” or elaborately rendered avatar skins and shapes, and animations enhancing gestures and movements such as a more natural-looking or more stylized walk. Since a resident is unlikely to seek information from a “newbie”, librarians and educators needed to provide both a traditional conservative humanoid appearance for newbies while also signaling to residents a sophisticated avatar appearance. Thus, an educator commented on his avatar appearance: “paid a good penny for it”, while a librarian described purchasing a higher-quality skin to better connect with residents: “I stuck with a noob skin for a long time [. . .] But my community is so sartorially obsessed, I finally decided it was leaving me out of an important cultural conversation with my main patron group”. Gender could easily be modified by changing an avatar’s shape, skin, and hair. A librarian discussed choosing between female and male self representations based on

 what might best fit expectations in a community of residents: “I have a basic female and basic male avatar... [the community] has an easier time with a nice bumbling male”. Another librarian described advantages of the female gender: “I have a male friend who uses a female alt at the ref desk because she is more ‘approachable’”. Ethnicity represented another performative choice. An educator said, “wherever I went I saw very few people of color [. . .] as avatars [. . .] I thought I’d do what His Holiness the Dalai Lama is reported to have said when asked what he’d come back as if he could choose his next incarnation. He reportedly said, ‘A woman of color’”. Librarians and educators sometimes used their own avatars in informational and learning scenarios. One librarian spoke of morphing appearance and gender to embody a historic figure in a re-enactment. An educator discussed using different “selves” for case study learning scenarios in which students observed a scene, deduced what had occurred, and determined next steps. Here, the avatar becomes part of the informational and learning environment.
5.2 Alternative identity
Additional virtual “selves” are created by adding new accounts, or “alts”. Five of the librarians and educators reported maintaining “alt” identities separate from their “professional” avatars. Less effort was generally invested in professional self presentation for “alts”. A librarian commented, “My alt has blue hair”. Another librarian said, “I don’t spend lindens or time fixing that avatar up” (“Lindens”, the virtual-world currency, are purchased with real-world money). The “alt” identity was sometimes used in work as an actor in an educational video, or a role-playing character in a teaching scenario. Work shifted to alts included backing up important inventory items, conducting research, and managing money. Alternate identity afforded a respite from being summoned into conversations. A librarian noted, “I started using another one [alt] just so I can come in and work in privacy”. The main avatar was “associated with all my inventory and contacts” but those same contacts could see whenever the librarian or educator logged in, sending instant messages (IMs) which interrupted planned work. Another librarian commented: “I am considering using [the alt] to work sometimes because I find now that I get so many IM’s while trying to work!”. However, the “alt” generally was not considered the “real” avatar self. An educator described the main avatar: “this one is ‘me’”. A librarian commented, “I feel weird coming inworld as my alt [. . .] it is like pretending!”. Identification with the “main” avatar sometimes included imbuing it with elements of real-life appearance. One librarian said, “I made my avatar look like my RL self”. An educator said, “I wanted it to look like myself as I knew I was going to be doing work here”. Only one study participant consistently used distancing language, speaking of the main avatar in third person: “I don’t really worry about his appearance too much” and referring to the avatar by name: “After a few moments of introspection and reflection, I changed [avatar name] back”. Other study participants placed greater emphasis on their main avatar’s appearance and role. As one educator stated, “my SL avatar is an extension of me into the virtual world”.
5.3 Dynamics of personal and working spaces
Just as the avatar was infinitely customizable, workplace surroundings could be customized to frame encounters. Librarians and educators often chose visual

 metaphors of a university classroom, campus, or library. Surrounding imagery of reference desks, classrooms, books, bookshelves and computers influenced expectations about professional roles and appropriate contextual behaviors. In observations of 50 libraries in Second Life, commonly observed workplace features included photos and posters (80 percent), books and bookshelves (68 percent), computers (56 percent), and reference desks (20 percent). A strong identification of libraries with books was seen not only in workplace imagery of books and bookshelves, but also in a librarian’s comment that a commonly asked patron question was, “Where are the books?”. For institutions within role-playing or themed communities (e.g. ancient Rome, the American Old West), workplace imagery might include a papyrus scroll instead of a book, or a steam-powered card catalog rather than a computer, but generally still conveyed scholarly purpose. While some virtual workplaces included books that could be read and computers that opened web pages when touched, in others a bookshelf or laptop served only as a non-functioning visual backdrop. The workplace setting contributed to, as one librarian put it, “the crucial importance of establishing trust”. A commonly reported user response among librarians was “people asking “are you really a librarian?” and “surprise at the fact that I am a ‘real’ librarian”. While some librarians and educators identified specific settings in which they worked (e.g. “an open courtyard with walls that are used for displays, and a labeled desk area”) others described ways of working not tied to any single place. An educator said, “There is no consistent setting”. Librarians spoke of doing their work “anywhere” and “everywhere” without the need for a specific location. The virtual world environment offered possibilities for conducting information interactions beyond traditional boundaries and familiar replications of real-world workplaces. An educator said, “I think it is just the beginning with folks going to what they are familiar with and then moving them to things and places that are not like real life and to places and things we cannot do in RL due to time and distance”. One example that librarians and educators described of breaking boundaries was real world limitations to serving only geographically local users. Within the virtual world, clientele changed by time of day – over 24 hours, the world came into the workplace with changing languages and timeframes of activity marking different nationalities of incoming users. Librarians designed information resources for an international clientele (“basic SL info in a couple of languages”) and carried translation tools for patrons speaking other languages. Both educators and librarians questioned rebuilding traditional settings in the virtual world environment. Educators spoke of teaching in open spaces such as “sandboxes” with plenty of room for students’ avatars. An educator commented: “a classroom is a classroom in RL or SL, and can be just as confining”. Librarians talked about departing from traditional library workplaces by placing reference desks outside of buildings, or tearing away walls and ceilings. A librarian noted: “When I was providing volunteer reference service, someone created a very futuristic looking reference desk. The funny thing was, no one ever used it. Not librarians. Not patrons”. Changing aesthetics, such as a garden of giant flowers replacing a botany laboratory, offer new challenges in asserting professional identity within workplaces not immediately recognizable as classrooms or libraries.

 6. Conclusions
This study found that librarians and educators faced the problem of commonly held perceptions of the virtual world as a game rather than a place for education and learning, a difficulty also discussed by Carr (2008) who referenced Goffman’s Frame Analysis (Goffman, 1974) in terms of how a game-like environment could influence students’ understandings, or misunderstandings, of the serious nature of in-world teaching and learning activities. In reframing the virtual world as an environment for professional activity, librarians and educators “set the scene” with workplace visual elements emphasizing educational purpose such as computers, books, and bookshelves. Stimpson (2009, p. 16) similarly found that computers were a typical workplace feature for in-world public libraries. However, traditional design motifs are observed beyond educational settings. Sociologist Nathaniel Glazer (2005) wrote of his surprise upon seeing throughout Second Life how “conservative and traditional the created spaces of the virtual world are”. For an avatar appearance that would be ‘taken seriously’, librarians and educators often chose traditional, conservative humanoid self-representations. Other research on avatars in business and sales confirms humanoid appearance as more “liked” by customers (Keeling et al., 2010). Librarians and educators purchased items for a higher-quality appearance in deference to expectations of Second Life residents, which echoed findings in research by Boostrom (2008, p. 17) on how purchased items enhanced identity for stigmatized newbies. Several other studies (Taylor, 1999; Messinger et al., 2008; Yee and Bailenson, 2007) emphasize the importance of attractive avatar appearance. In research by Taylor (1999, p. 441) one participant summed up the impact of avatar identity as performative choice: “people treat you based on how you present yourself, and, if you pay attention, you’ll notice that *you * change depending on how you present yourself”. Second Life has recently responded to researcher-identified stigmatization of new users by introducing a key modification – regularly changing, higher-quality avatars for incoming users, making it more difficult to visually identify new users. Alternative identity was sometimes used in creating a separate “self” for fulfilling tasks, with five of the librarians and educators (42 percent) reporting that they maintained an “alt” identity. The New Media Consortium (2007, p. 5) found that among 207 educators and librarians surveyed, 33 percent created two or more Second Life accounts. Alternative identity can extend to other virtual worlds as well. Taylor (1999, pp. 439-40) refers to “plural existence” in which users have digital selves in not just one, but multiple virtual worlds. Second Life is one among hundreds of virtual worlds in existence. Although the extent of mainstream adoption of virtual worlds remains to be seen, some trends toward increasing use can be observed. During 2009, Second Life users logged 481 million in-world hours and spent $US567m dollars on virtual goods and services, and in December 2009 traffic increased upon release of James Cameron’s blockbuster movie Avatar (Linden, 2010a; Linden, 2010b). Open source virtual world software options coupled with recent price increases by Linden Labs sparked a diaspora to other virtual worlds such as ReactionGrid (see http://reactiongrid.com/). Meanwhile, game-based learning continues to gain traction among educators (Annetta et al., 2010) as children grow up with online games and virtual worlds.

 Traffic of children into virtual worlds is driven by corporations bundling virtual world logins with children’s toys such as Webkinz stuffed animals, Bratz dolls and Barbie dolls. Millions of young users play and socialize together in Runescape, with 4.5 million users in 2009, Club Penguin, with 4.6 million unique visitors in March 2008 alone, and other settings (Buckleitner, 2008; Snider and Molina, 2009). Steinkuehler and Williams (2006, p. 886) described online games as potential “third places”, while Crowe and Bradford (2006, p. 337) suggested that parents may perceive virtual public spaces as safer for children than physical public spaces. For young users, growing up with gaming and virtual worlds may raise future expectations of more visually engaging and immersive experiences within online libraries and online education. A continuing challenge for librarians and educators is in evolving best practices for avatar-mediated interactions. One librarian in this study described “a hybrid of library and museum techniques” allowing “the interplay of words and images to tell the story” within virtual world workplaces. While it remains too soon to speak of universally-accepted best practices, the work of early adopters provides foundational groundwork toward establishing professional standards and new media literacies in librarianship and education within virtual worlds.
